bins.name: Advanced conductors as standard study option
bins.desc: CTC requests that transmission providers explicitly evaluate reconductoring with commercially available advanced conductors as a standard alternative in interconnection and planning studies. The company argues this approach directly advances FERC's objectives by minimizing costly network upgrades and ensuring timely interconnection.
quotes: Reconductoring with advanced conductors is a proven, fast, and cost-effective way to create transmission headroom on existing corridors.

Carbon composite core advanced conductors can double the capacity on existing structures because they do not have the increase in weight and sag necessary when capacity is increased with early and mid-1900s steel core conductors like ACSR and ACSS.

Should FERC decide to invoke authority over the interconnection of large loads greater than 20 megawatts (MW), we urge the Commission to require transmission providers to explicitly evaluate reconductoring with commercially available advanced conductors as a standard alternative in interconnection and planning studies before ordering more extensive network upgrades.

Southern California Edison reconductored 137 miles of its 230 kV Big Creek transmission corridor with ACCC Conductor, avoiding a costly rebuild while eliminating sag violations and adding more than 60% additional line capacity. The project cut costs from $135 million to $48 million, reduced completion time from 48 to 18 months, improved safety, lowered line losses by 30%, and delivered significant environmental and customer savings.

bins.name: Significant cost reduction through reconductoring
bins.desc: Advanced conductors substantially reduce interconnection costs compared to new transmission construction. CTC highlights the Southern California Edison case, where reconductoring with advanced conductors reduced project costs from 135 million to 48 million dollars (65 percent savings) while increasing capacity by 60 percent in 18 months.
quotes: Reconductoring with advanced conductors is a proven, fast, and cost-effective way to create transmission headroom on existing corridors.

Carbon composite core advanced conductors can double the capacity on existing structures because they do not have the increase in weight and sag necessary when capacity is increased with early and mid-1900s steel core conductors like ACSR and ACSS.

Southern California Edison reconductored 137 miles of its 230 kV Big Creek transmission corridor with ACCC Conductor, avoiding a costly rebuild while eliminating sag violations and adding more than 60% additional line capacity. The project cut costs from $135 million to $48 million, reduced completion time from 48 to 18 months, improved safety, lowered line losses by 30%, and delivered significant environmental and customer savings.
Reconductoring with advanced conductors costs far less than building new lines—savings that translate into lower electricity bills for American families and reduced energy costs for businesses.

bins.name: Rapid capacity addition critical for large load integration
bins.desc: Speed is essential to interconnecting large loads and connecting waiting generation capacity. CTC emphasizes that reconductoring with advanced conductors can double transmission capacity in 6 to 24 months by reusing towers and rights-of-way, which is necessary to unlock the 2.6 terawatts of generation currently in interconnection queues.
quotes: This speed-to-power approach is key when discussing steps that can and must be taken in order to add new large loads to the power system.
Reconductoring existing lines with advanced conductors can often double capacity in 6-24 months by reusing towers and rights-of-way, and avoiding many siting reviews.
This is critical to connecting existing generation to demand, as well as unleashing the 2.6 Terawatts of generation capacity currently sitting in interconnection queues.
